currentUse: The 1903 church is gone — demolished after repeated arsons following its 1970 sale; its Latin-inscribed cornerstone survives embedded in a small warehouse-like basement-only building on the site. The former parish school on Tabor Street (which served as the worship space 1962-1993 and pastoral center until 1997) still stands; it was sold to the Sons of God church in 1997 and, per a November 2023 account, still shows remnants of the "St. Vincent De Paul" name but appears inactive as a congregation.
summary: St. Vincent de Paul, the Lithuanian parish of Pittsburgh's Esplen neighborhood, no longer exists in any form: its original 1903 church was closed for worship in 1962, sold out of diocesan hands in 1970, and later demolished after numerous arsons, with only its Latin-inscribed cornerstone surviving on a small warehouse-like replacement structure. Worship had moved in 1962 into a remodeled hall in the parish school on Tabor Street, and the parish itself was suppressed in 1993, when population loss led the Diocese of Pittsburgh to merge it with six McKees Rocks parishes into the new St. John of God Parish. The school-church became a pastoral center, which was closed in 1997 and sold shortly afterward to the Sons of God church; that building still stood as of November 2023, one of the few substantial structures left in a neighborhood of frame houses and vacant lots, with traces of the St. Vincent de Paul name but no Lithuanian inscriptions remaining. The canonical successor chain has since consolidated further: St. John of God was merged on January 6, 2020 into Archangel Gabriel Parish (with Holy Trinity, Robinson Township and St. Malachy, Kennedy Township) under the diocese's On Mission for The Church Alive! restructuring, and that parish closed Mother of Sorrows Church in McKees Rocks on July 1, 2022. No Lithuanian community activity, preservation effort, or reunion connected to the Esplen parish was found in recent press.

developments.headline: Parish suppressed in eight-way McKees Rocks-area merger
developments.detail: Citing population losses, the Diocese of Pittsburgh merged St. Vincent de Paul, Esplen with Ss. Cyril & Methodius, St. Francis de Sales, St. Maria Goretti, St. Mark, St. Mary Help of Christians and Mother of Sorrows of McKees Rocks in March 1993 to form the new St. John of God Parish. The Esplen church (the converted school hall) became a pastoral center.

sources.title: Saint Vincent de Paul, Esplen — Parish History
Saint John of God, McKees Rocks — Parish History

developments.headline: Pastoral center closed and sold to the Sons of God church
developments.detail: The former school building on Tabor Street, which had served as the parish's church from 1962 and then as a pastoral center after the 1993 merger, was closed in 1997 and sold shortly afterward to the Sons of God church. Remnants of the 'St. Vincent De Paul' name remain visible on the building; no Lithuanian inscriptions survive.

sources.title: Saint Vincent de Paul, Esplen — Parish History

developments.headline: Original 1903 church demolished after repeated arsons; cornerstone survives
developments.detail: The 1903 church, closed for worship in 1962 and sold in 1970, was demolished after numerous arsons. Its cornerstone, with a Latin inscription, remains at a corner of a small warehouse-like basement-only building on the site. No press coverage giving the demolition year, buyer, or sale price was found.

sources.title: Pittsburgh area: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Successor parish St. John of God merged into Archangel Gabriel Parish
developments.detail: Under the diocese's On Mission for The Church Alive! restructuring, St. John of God (McKees Rocks) was formally merged with Holy Trinity (Robinson Township) and St. Malachy (Kennedy Township) on January 6, 2020 to create Archangel Gabriel Parish — the current canonical home of the former Esplen parish's lineage. On July 1, 2022 the new parish closed Mother of Sorrows Church in McKees Rocks; three churches remain open.

sources.title: Archangel Gabriel, McKees Rocks — Parish History

developments.headline: Former parish school photographed still standing in a hollowed-out Esplen
developments.detail: The Father Pitt photo blog documented the former St. Vincent de Paul School still standing — one of the few substantial structures in a neighborhood otherwise of frame houses and vacant lots — noting the church and other parish buildings are gone and that the school's later non-denominational church use appears inactive.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions, but two significant refinements. (1) The archive record's "demolished pre-2017 after repeated arson; only the cornerstone survives" is confirmed by Global True Lithuania, but it applies only to the original 1903 church — the former parish school on Tabor Street, which was the parish's actual worship space from 1962 to 1993, still stood as of November 2023, so a substantial parish building does survive. (2) The archive leaves the closure date unstated: the diocesan history establishes the sequence — old church closed for worship 1962, sold out of diocesan hands 1970, parish canonically merged into St. John of God in 1993, pastoral center closed and sold 1997. The 1970 sale means the demolished church had left diocesan ownership decades before the parish ended, a nuance for the ownership narrative. Lithuanian-parish identity in Esplen is confirmed by the diocese's own history (founded by Lithuanian immigrants who had been attending St. Casimir on the South Side), so there is no ethnic-parish ambiguity.
gaps: No source gives the year the 1903 church was actually demolished, who owned it at demolition, or any sale price for the 1970 church sale or 1997 school sale — searches for arson/demolition press coverage returned nothing specific to this building. The exact street address of the demolished church was not verified (the school is on Tabor Street per Global True Lithuania). Current (2024-2026) status of the former school building — whether the Sons of God congregation or any successor still owns or uses it — is unverified; the newest eyewitness account is the November 2023 Father Pitt post describing its church use as apparently inactive. Allegheny County property records were not consulted. The live diopitt.org and archangelgabrielparish.org pages are behind Cloudflare and were read via Wayback Machine snapshots (2023-2024), so any post-snapshot edits to the diocesan histories would not be reflected. No Lithuanian community reunions, preservation efforts, or Draugas-adjacent press about this parish were found in recent years.
